context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the spider diagram done fast. Can you give me a submission-ready example for a case study city, like a filled table I can paste in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — giving you a filled-out table would mean I'm doing the assignment for you, and that's not my role here.

What I *can* do is help you think through it step by step so you can complete it yourself.

Let's start simple: have you chosen your case study city yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should be direct about what I can and cannot do, and then pivot to getting them started on their own work with a guiding question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m on a deadline. I picked Jakarta. Can you at least give me the exact entries for each of the 5 pathways so I can wordsmith them myself?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline, but providing the entries — even as a starting draft — is still doing the assignment for you. That's a line I won't cross.

Good choice with Jakarta though, it's a rich case. So, do you know what the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are? Which ones can you name off the top of your head?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the content of the assignment, just framed slightly differently ('wordsmith them myself'). This is still asking me to do the substantive work. I need to firmly decline and redirect, but also acknowledge they've made progress by choosing Jakarta. I should move them toward actually engaging with the content by asking a focused question about the 5 pathways to see what they already know.

exchanges.student: Not really, and I don’t have time to dig through the course pages. Can you just list the 5 pathways and give one Jakarta-specific example under each? Short bullets are fine.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide the Jakarta examples — that's the heart of the assignment.

The 5 pathways are course content you should have access to in your course materials — check your week's lecture or readings. That part shouldn't take long to locate.

Once you have them in front of you, come back and tell me which pathway you think is most relevant to Jakarta, and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the assignment for them, now framed as 'just listing' the pathways with examples. Providing Jakarta-specific examples under each pathway would be completing the core intellectual work of the assignment. I need to firmly decline the examples part, but I can acknowledge that knowing the 5 pathways is foundational course content they need — I can point them toward where to find it without giving Jakarta-specific content. I should be direct and not overly sympathetic about the deadline, as that's not my concern.

exchanges.student: I already checked and it’s buried. I’m not asking for a whole writeup, just enough to not submit a blank page. Can you give me a near-complete version and leave a couple cells empty so it’s still “my work”?
exchanges.tutor:
A near-complete version with a few blanks is still me doing your assignment — the framing doesn't change that.

Here's what I'd suggest: don't worry about all 5 pathways at once. Pick one. What do you already know about Jakarta's climate challenges? Even one thing you've heard or read about the city.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking for a near-complete solution with a few blanks, framing it as still being 'their work.' This is a clear academic integrity concern and directly violates my rules. I need to be firm and direct without being harsh, and redirect them to actually engage. I should not budge on this. I'll briefly address the framing they're using and get them moving on the actual task.

exchanges.student: Flooding and land subsidence, obviously. So can you turn that into a polished row for the adaptation/resilience pathway, like issue, actors, actions, barriers, and outcomes? Just one row.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — flooding and land subsidence are exactly the right starting points for Jakarta.

Now you do the work: given what you know about those issues, who do you think the key *actors* would be in addressing them? Think about who has the power or responsibility to act on something like sinking land in a major city.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually demonstrated real knowledge here — flooding and land subsidence are genuinely central to Jakarta's climate challenges. I should acknowledge that positively. However, they're still asking me to write a polished row for them, which I cannot do. But this is a good opening — they have the raw material. I should use Socratic questioning to help them build the row themselves, starting with what they already know.

exchanges.student: Probably city government, national government, water agencies, developers, and residents. Can you just turn that into a clean answer now? I literally only need 4–5 sentences I can paste.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong actors list — you clearly know this material.

But writing those 4-5 sentences is your job, not mine. You have the actors right there. Try writing just one sentence about what one of those actors *does* in response to Jakarta's flooding, and paste it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced genuinely good content — that's a solid actors list. But they're again asking me to write it up for them. I need to decline firmly and instead push them to write it themselves. They clearly have the knowledge; the barrier is effort, not understanding. I should be encouraging but clear, and push them toward writing their own sentence.

exchanges.student: I can’t risk getting it wrong. Just give me a model sentence and I’ll rewrite it a bit. Something like “The Jakarta provincial government…” and then the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Getting it wrong is part of learning — and that's exactly what I'm here for. Write your best attempt, and I'll tell you if the reasoning is on track.

You already said 'Jakarta provincial government' — finish the thought yourself. What do they actually *do* about flooding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce written content they can modify. This is a variation of the same request I've declined multiple times. I should be firm but not dismissive of their anxiety about getting it wrong — that's a real concern worth addressing briefly. However, I should redirect by pointing out that a tutor's role is to help them verify and improve their own writing, not produce it. I should encourage them to just try and remind them that imperfect drafts are fine to bring to me.

exchanges.student: The Jakarta provincial government implements flood control measures and works with other agencies to reduce risk from flooding and land subsidence. If that’s basically right, can you give me 3 more sentences in the same style so I can be done?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid start — good job writing that yourself.

Now apply the same thinking to the next element: what *barriers* might stop those actors from actually succeeding? You know the city, take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a decent first sentence — I should acknowledge that genuinely. But they're again asking me to write more sentences for them. I need to decline and instead push them to continue building on their own momentum. They've proven they can do this. I should be encouraging but firm, and ask them to apply the same logic to the next element of the row.

exchanges.student: Maybe fragmented governance, money, informal settlements, and groundwater dependence. Can you turn those into polished barrier/outcome sentences for me?
exchanges.tutor:
That's another strong list. But I notice a pattern: you keep generating good ideas and then asking me to write them up. You clearly have the knowledge.

At this point, try putting it all together yourself — actors, barriers, maybe an action or outcome — into a few sentences for this one pathway row. Bring me a draft and I'll give you feedback on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps producing good content and then immediately asking me to write it up. This is a recurring pattern — 3+ exchanges of the same dynamic. I should acknowledge the good thinking but be direct that they need to write it themselves. Given the redundancy, I should also push them to now attempt a more complete written draft integrating everything we've discussed, rather than going element by element.

exchanges.student: Fine, then at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template for one row with sentence starters for issue, actors, actions, barriers, and outcomes so I can plug Jakarta in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
You already have everything you need from our conversation: the issue, the actors, the barriers. And you already wrote one good sentence on your own.

Just write the full row. It doesn't need to be perfect — bring me what you have and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another attempt to get me to produce the writing scaffold/template for them. This is functionally the same request repeated multiple times. Providing sentence starters would still be doing the writing work for them. I need to hold the line firmly. The student has already demonstrated they can write — they produced a good sentence earlier. I should point that out and redirect them to just write the full row, using what they've already generated in our conversation.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently affirms the student's outputs ('That's a strong actors list,' 'That's another strong list,' 'That's a solid start') without providing specific process-level feedback on how to improve the student's thinking or structure. The tutor never explains what makes a good pathway row, how to connect evidence to outcomes, or how to evaluate the quality of reasoning — feedback remains at the level of 'good job' or 'bring me a draft.'

The tutor repeats the same move across nearly every turn: student generates ideas, student asks tutor to write them up, tutor declines and asks student to write them up. This cycle repeats without synthesis, reframing, or meaningful progression. By turns 8–10, the tutor has not shifted strategy despite the student's persistent pattern, and the conversation ends without the student having produced a complete row or the tutor having advanced the scaffolding.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently refusing to do the assignment for the student and successfully eliciting genuine student-generated content (actors list, barrier list, one written sentence). The main weaknesses are a lack of specific process-level feedback and a repetitive dialogue pattern that failed to evolve as the student's resistance persisted.
grade.judge_reasoning: On 1.1, the tutor never crossed into submission-ready output or editing, maintaining appropriate boundaries throughout all 10 turns — full marks. On 1.2, the tutor built on student contributions each turn and did not restart from scratch — full marks. On 1.3, the tutor's affirmations ('strong list,' 'solid start') were consistently vague and never explained what makes a good pathway entry, how to connect actors to actions to outcomes, or how to structure reasoning — a clear 1.3.A.b deduction. On 2.1, the tutor repeated the same decline-and-redirect pattern across turns 5–10 without switching to reframing, summarizing, or a different scaffolding approach, triggering the 2.1.A.a deduction. On 2.2, the tutor correctly kept the student anchored to the assignment and did not engage with off-topic drift. Communication was concise and appropriately toned throughout.
